movements.notes: Mobilize hip capsule, increase synovial fluid
Spinal segmentation, thoracic and lumbar mobility
Hip flexor and external rotator stretch
Prime squat pattern, activate glutes and quads
Posterior chain activation, hinge pattern rehearsal
Dynamic hamstring stretch, shoulder stability

Wave loading: oscillate between heavier and lighter loads to exploit post-activation potentiation. First wave: 75-80-85%, second wave: 75-82-87%. Heavy sets prime nervous system for subsequent performance.
Moderate load to preserve technique under fatigue
Strict or kipping acceptable if athlete has 3+ strict pull-up capacity

metcon.target_score: Complete all 5 rounds as fast as possible. Target: 8-10 minutes for experienced athletes.

movements.notes: Reduce perceived soreness post-squat volume
Address hip flexor tightness from squatting
Hip external rotator release, parasympathetic recovery

session_why.text: Heavy strength work placed first to ensure CNS freshness and technique quality under load. Short metcon (<12 min time cap) targets glycolytic system without excessive systemic fatigue that would interfere with strength adaptation. Hinge pattern (deadlift) in metcon complements but does not overload the posterior chain after heavy squats.
session_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
session_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movement_why.text: Back squat is a primary lower body strength movement targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and spinal erectors. Deadlift in the metcon provides hinge pattern balance while remaining submaximal (60% 1RM). Pull-ups ensure weekly vertical pulling volume and upper body muscular endurance stimulus. This session addresses squat and hinge patterns as required weekly movement balance.

loading_why.text: Wave loading (75-80-85%, then 75-82-87%) exploits post-activation potentiation: heavy sets prime the nervous system, making subsequent moderate loads feel lighter and allowing higher total volume at elevated intensities. This scheme is appropriate for Week 2-3 intensity phases and experienced athletes. Rest periods of 3 minutes ensure full phosphocreatine recovery between heavy sets.
loading_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 19(2)

movements.notes: Controlled Articular Rotations for shoulder capsule health
Improve thoracic extension and rotation for front rack position
Hip capsule mobility preparation
Squat pattern activation
Front-loaded squat prep, reinforce upright torso
Hip flexor, hamstring, and thoracic mobility complex
EMOM format: perform 3 front squats at the start of every 2 minutes. If reps take 20 seconds, you rest 100 seconds. Maintains pacing discipline and session flow.
Hip hinge power, posterior chain endurance
Monostructural cardio, aerobic power development
Horizontal push pattern, muscular endurance

metcon.target_score: Complete as many rounds + reps as possible in 12 minutes. Target: 6-8 rounds for experienced athletes.

movements.notes: Release quadriceps tension post-squat
Upper back soft tissue work
Hip external rotator and flexor release

session_why.text: Front squat emphasizes anterior core, quadriceps, and upright posture under load. EMOM format keeps the strength block time-efficient and mentally engaging. Moderate-duration AMRAP (12 min) targets glycolytic and oxidative systems without excessive fatigue. Session includes squat pattern (strength), hinge pattern (KB swing), and monostructural cardio (assault bike) for weekly movement balance.

movement_why.text: Front squat develops quadriceps strength and core stability required for clean receiving positions. Kettlebell swings provide posterior chain power and hinge pattern volume. Push-ups ensure horizontal push volume (weekly push:pull ratio requirement). Assault bike delivers monostructural aerobic stimulus required weekly.

loading_why.text: E2MOM format at 78% 1RM provides sufficient phosphocreatine recovery between sets (2 minutes meets the 2-3 minute guideline for heavy loading). Auto-regulated rest rewards efficient execution. This format is appropriate for Week 2 moderate-heavy loading and keeps athletes engaged with the timer. AMRAP pacing is self-regulated, targeting aerobic power and muscular endurance.

wods.title: Ramp to Heavy Deadlift + Short For Time

movements.notes: Spinal segmentation and mobility
Hip capsule preparation
Upper back soft tissue prep for deadlift positioning
Hinge pattern activation, posterior chain prep
Glute activation prior to heavy pulling
Dynamic hamstring stretch and core activation

Ramp/build-up progression: progressively increase load while decreasing reps. Prepares nervous system for heavier loads, develops technique under progressively challenging weights, reduces injury risk. Final single at 88% is technical max, not true 1RM.
Lower body power, plyometric stimulus. Exploits PAP from heavy deadlifts.
Full-body conditioning, glycolytic demand

metcon.target_score: Complete all reps as fast as possible. Target: 6-8 minutes for experienced athletes.

movements.notes: Release anterior leg tension from box jumps
Address hip flexor tightness from burpees
Hip external rotator release

session_why.text: Heavy deadlift provides maximal posterior chain strength stimulus. Ramp progression allows neural preparation and technique refinement under progressively challenging loads. Short metcon (<10 min) exploits post-activation potentiation from heavy pulling: box jumps benefit from temporarily enhanced neuromuscular output. Time domain targets phosphocreatine and early glycolytic systems.
session_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 19(2)

movement_why.text: Deadlift is the primary hinge pattern movement, developing posterior chain strength (glutes, hamstrings, spinal erectors). Box jumps provide lower body power and plyometric training. Burpees ensure full-body conditioning and cardiovascular demand. This session addresses hinge pattern requirement and power development across the 10 physical skills.

loading_why.text: Ramp/build-up progression (65-72-78-83-88%) is appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment and complex movement neural preparation. Progressive warm-up sets are essential for neural preparation and technique refinement under load. This scheme reduces injury risk by gradually approaching working sets and is suitable for all athlete levels.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training

wods.title: Top Set Strict Press + EMOM Metcon

movements.notes: Shoulder capsule controlled articular rotations
Shoulder mobility and range of motion prep
Improve thoracic extension for overhead pressing
Posterior shoulder and scapular activation
General upper body pressing activation
Movement-specific warmup for overhead pressing

movements.load: Set 1: 2 reps @ 85% 1RM (top set), Sets 2-5: 5 reps @ 68% 1RM (back-off volume)
movements.load_rpe: Top set RPE 9, volume sets RPE 7 if 1RM unknown
movements.rest: 3 minutes after top set, 90 seconds between volume sets
movements.notes: Top set + back-off volume: one heavy set (85% for 2 reps) followed by multiple lighter volume sets (68% for 5 reps). Combines neural stimulation with hypertrophy volume efficiently.
Minute 1, 4, 7, 10, 13: Row 12 calories
Minute 2, 5, 8, 11, 14: 10 goblet squats
Minute 3, 6, 9, 12, 15: 15 push-ups

metcon.target_score: Complete all prescribed reps within each minute. Rest remainder of minute. Aim to finish each station in 30-40 seconds.

movements.notes: Upper back soft tissue release
Restore shoulder range of motion post-pressing
Hip mobility and parasympathetic recovery

session_why.text: Strict press develops upper body vertical pushing strength and shoulder stability. Top set + back-off volume scheme allows high-intensity neural work followed by structural hypertrophy work in a time-efficient format. EMOM metcon ensures consistent output and pacing discipline, targeting aerobic power with manageable work:rest ratios.

movement_why.text: Strict press is the foundational vertical push movement for shoulder strength. Rowing provides monostructural cardio and horizontal pulling (contributes to weekly push:pull balance). Goblet squats maintain squat pattern volume without posterior chain overload. Push-ups add horizontal push volume. Session addresses vertical push, horizontal push/pull, and squat patterns.

loading_why.text: Top set + back-off volume (85% for 2 reps, then 68% for 5×5) combines peak intensity neural stimulation with volume accumulation for hypertrophy. This is appropriate for Week 3 intensity peak and intermediate athletes. Heavy top set happens fresh; volume work follows with reduced CNS demand. This scheme minimizes interference while maximizing adaptation diversity in concurrent training.

movements.notes: Hip capsule mobility
Shoulder capsule preparation for front rack
Thoracic mobility for catch position
Hinge pattern activation
Hip extension power primer
Movement-specific warmup, technique rehearsal

Straight sets at moderate load. Emphasize bar speed and technical precision. Olympic lifts require full neural availability; moderate load (72%) allows volume accumulation while maintaining quality. Focus on aggressive hip extension and fast elbows.
Monostructural cardio, coordination skill
Vertical pulling volume, upper back and lat development
Vertical pushing under fatigue, moderate load

metcon.target_score: Complete all 4 rounds as fast as possible. Target: 10-12 minutes for experienced athletes.

movements.notes: Upper back soft tissue work after pulling volume
Hip flexor release post-Olympic lifting
Hip external rotator and parasympathetic recovery

session_why.text: Olympic lifting (hang power clean) placed first for CNS freshness and technical quality. Moderate RFT metcon (14 min time cap) targets glycolytic and mixed energy systems without excessive fatigue. Session includes Olympic lift, vertical push/pull balance, and monostructural cardio for weekly movement diversity.

movement_why.text: Hang power clean develops explosive hip extension, rate of force development, and pulling power. Chest-to-bar pull-ups provide vertical pulling volume (weekly push:pull balance). Push press adds vertical pushing under fatigue. Double-unders fulfill monostructural cardio requirement and develop coordination. This session addresses Olympic lifting, push/pull balance, and monostructural patterns.

loading_why.text: Straight sets at 72% 1RM are appropriate for Week 1-2 Olympic lifting phases. This scheme emphasizes technical quality and bar speed over maximal loading. Olympic lifts degrade rapidly under fatigue; moderate intensity (72%) with adequate rest (2 min) preserves movement quality across all sets. Simpler progression scheme reduces cognitive load and allows focus on explosive execution.
